title: Tax Write Offs for House Flippers: Complete List to Maximize Your Profits
excerpt: House flippers leave thousands of dollars on the table every year by missing legitimate tax deductions. This complete list of tax write offs for house flippers breaks down every deduction you can claim to keep more money in your pocket.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: tax write offs for house flippers complete list
real estate investor tax write offs
metaDescription: Complete list of tax write offs for house flippers. Learn every deduction you can claim in 2024 to reduce your tax bill and maximize flip profits.
metaKeywords: tax write offs for house flippers complete list, house flipping tax deductions, real estate investor tax write offs, fix and flip tax deductions, flipping houses taxes, real estate tax deductions 2024

sections.content: Flipping houses can be incredibly profitable, but taxes can take a massive bite out of your gains. Many flippers pay 25% to 40% or more in combined federal, state, and self-employment taxes if they are not careful. The good news is that the IRS allows real estate investors to deduct a wide range of business expenses that can significantly lower that tax bill. Knowing the tax write offs for house flippers complete list is not just helpful — it is essential for running a profitable business.
Flipping Houses and the IRS: Are You a Dealer or an Investor?

Before diving into deductions, you need to understand how the IRS classifies you. If you flip houses regularly, the IRS likely considers you a dealer in real estate, not a passive investor. That means your profits are treated as ordinary income and are also subject to self-employment tax (currently 15.3% on the first $160,200 of net earnings in 2024). This classification actually opens up more deduction opportunities, because you are treated as running a business. Most active flippers should consider forming an LLC or S-Corp to manage their tax liability. Always consult a CPA familiar with real estate before making structural decisions.
The goal is not to cheat on your taxes — it is to pay exactly what you owe and not a penny more. Knowing your deductions is how professionals do that. — Common wisdom among real estate CPAs
The Complete List of Tax Write Offs for House Flippers
1. Purchase Price and Acquisition Costs

The price you pay for a property is not directly deducted — it is added to your cost basis. However, all costs tied to acquiring the property reduce your taxable profit when you sell. This includes your purchase price, title search fees, recording fees, transfer taxes, and any legal fees paid at closing. If you paid $120,000 for a property and spent $3,500 in closing costs, your cost basis starts at $123,500.
2. Renovation and Rehab Costs

This is often the single largest deduction for house flippers. Every dollar you spend rehabbing a property — materials, labor, permits, dumpster rentals, structural repairs — reduces your taxable gain dollar for dollar. Whether you are updating a kitchen in Atlanta or rewiring a bungalow in Phoenix, keep every receipt. If you are estimating rehab costs before you buy, use the free [Rehab Estimator at aiwholesail.com/tools/rehab-estimator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/rehab-estimator) to get accurate numbers that also help you project your real tax exposure.
3. Holding Costs

Every month a property sits in your inventory, you are racking up expenses. All of these holding costs are deductible. If you carry a property for six months and pay $1,200 per month in mortgage interest, $300 in insurance, $400 in property taxes, and $150 in utilities, that is $12,300 in holding costs you can write off against your profit. Active markets like [Dallas, TX](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx) and [Phoenix, AZ](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) move fast, but even a 90-day hold adds up quickly.

items: Mortgage interest on the flip property
Property taxes during the hold period

sections.content: 4. Financing and Loan Costs

Hard money loans are the lifeblood of many flipping operations, and the fees are fully deductible. Origination points, loan fees, and interest paid on hard money or private money loans all reduce your taxable gain. If you took out a $150,000 hard money loan at 3 points, that $4,500 in origination fees alone is a deductible expense. Keep detailed statements from your lender to back this up.
5. Selling Costs

When you sell the property, the costs of the sale itself reduce your taxable profit. These include real estate agent commissions (typically 5% to 6%), staging costs, professional photography, closing costs paid by the seller, and any buyer concessions. On a $300,000 sale, a 6% commission alone is $18,000 coming off your taxable gain.
6. Business Operating Expenses

Running a flipping business comes with overhead, and almost all of it is deductible. These are expenses tied to operating your business, not any single property. If you are new to structuring a real estate business, our guide on [Wholesaling Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesaling-real-estate-for-beginners) covers the foundational setup steps that also apply to flippers.

items: Vehicle mileage or actual auto expenses for business travel (67 cents per mile in 2024)
Real estate software, subscriptions, and apps
Marketing costs — direct mail, bandit signs, online ads
Education — courses, books, seminars related to real estate investing
Business meals (50% deductible when discussing business)
Business entity fees and annual filing costs

sections.content: 7. Contractor and Professional Fees

Any professional you hire to help run your business is deductible. This includes your CPA, real estate attorney, property inspector, and even a business coach if their work is specifically related to your real estate investing activities. Make sure you issue 1099-NEC forms to any non-corporate contractor you pay $600 or more in a calendar year. Failing to do so can jeopardize your deduction if you are ever audited.

Pro Tip: Create a dedicated business checking account and credit card for all flip-related expenses. This single habit saves hours at tax time and makes your deductions nearly bulletproof if you are ever audited. Never mix personal and business transactions.
8. Self-Employment Tax Deduction

If you are a dealer and pay self-employment tax, you can deduct half of it on your personal tax return. This is an above-the-line deduction, meaning it reduces your adjusted gross income even if you do not itemize. If you owe $12,000 in self-employment tax, you can deduct $6,000 directly from your income.
9. Retirement Contributions

Self-employed flippers can contribute to a SEP-IRA, Solo 401(k), or SIMPLE IRA. A SEP-IRA allows you to contribute up to 25% of your net self-employment income, up to $69,000 in 2024. These contributions are fully tax-deductible and also build long-term wealth. This is one of the most powerful and underused tools available to active flippers.
10. Health Insurance Premiums

If you are self-employed and not eligible for coverage through a spouse's employer plan, you can deduct 100% of health insurance premiums for yourself and your family. This includes medical, dental, and qualified long-term care insurance. This deduction alone can be worth $5,000 to $25,000 depending on your family size and plan.
What House Flippers Cannot Deduct

Not everything is deductible. Personal expenses disguised as business expenses are the number one audit trigger. You also cannot take depreciation on flip properties the way a landlord can, because flipping inventory is not considered a depreciable long-term asset. The IRS specifically prohibits the Section 1031 exchange for dealer properties, so you cannot defer your gains on a flip the way a rental property investor could. Understanding these boundaries keeps you protected.
Tracking Your Deductions: Build the Habit Now

The best deduction strategy means nothing if you cannot prove the expenses. Use accounting software like QuickBooks or a real estate-specific tool to log every expense by property. Take photos of every receipt and upload them immediately. Create a folder for each flip — physical or digital — and store all contracts, invoices, and bank statements there. Flippers working in active markets like [Atlanta, GA](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga) are often juggling two or three properties at a time, and organization is the only way to track it all accurately. If you are also exploring wholesaling as a way to generate deal flow, check out our detailed guide on [How to Wholesale Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesale-real-estate-beginners-guide) to understand how deal sourcing and tax treatment differ between wholesaling and flipping.
